Theme Song
Last month's issue about the similarities of learning email and Roosh's (the guy who
picks up chicks) guide to learning his skillz got quite a reaction. Apparently, many of y'all
find the topic as fascinating as most other guys do. You know, it's funny, but sometimes I
think about doing a serious search for someone who is really ugly but can still pick up
women. Why? Because selling a product in that market knowing just a few basic email
skillz would be like selling the Ring of Power to Gollum. That market is red hot. And the
way most of that community has no real knowledge about how to sell (in my humble, but
accurate, opinion) means the market is RIPE for the picking. I don't know this for a fact

Gust a suspicion) but I bet that market is even hotter and more rabid than weight loss or
gol£
Anyway, on to bid'niz.
Since y'allliked that bit last month, how about more of the same?
About a year or so ago, one of my friends (who also finds the whole "game" deal
fascinating -- as do many smart marketers) sent me a movie on the subject he said I HAD
to see. He said it was one of the best movies he'd seen on the topic and that it had a lot of
real-life selling applications. So he sent it to me, I watched it and, yeah, he was right. It's
got a great 3-part way of selling that is perfect for email marketing. In fact, I would say
the 3 part method makes up the anatomy of most of my highest selling emails.
What is the movie called?
"The Tao Of Steve"
Very groovy movie.
Kind of "B movie" quality. And, quite hokey at times. But if you look at it through
the eyes of a marketer and not as entertainment (although it is entertaining) you can pick
some huge, fatty nuggets of gold up and use in your emails, blogs, podcasts, videos,
articles and any other kind of online selling. It's that universally applicable. (Oh, and it
will also help you with selling yourself to women, for ye men who are so inclined to do
so...)
OK, so am I ever gonna reveal this 3-part system?
Or, am I gonna pull the rug here and tell you to watch the movie?
Hey! Of COURSE I'm gonna tell it to you. That's why I'm here, Stud. So, in a
nutshell, following is the 3 part formula. And then, we'll break each part down so you can
apply it IMMEDIATELY to your emails. Ka-PEESH?
OK, then, here it is:
-2-

-- 2 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
1. Be desire-less
2. Be excellent
3. Be gone
My but that's simple, isn't it?
However, it also may not make much sense if you haven't seen the movie. So let's
break this down piece by piece and apply it to email:
1. Be Desire-less
In the movie, the idea is, if you want to get a woman, you can't go around
telegraphing your desire for her. Just the opposite! You have to behave (and believe it) like
getting it on with her is not on your mind at all, and you just want to hang out or
whatever. No pressure, no hidden tricks or giving off a vibe you're "up to something."
That gets the woman's guard down (and, let's face it, many women are swimming in a sea
of guys who only want to have sex with them, so it's a perfectly natural guard to have up).
Well, guess what?
It's the exact same thing when selling with email. Very rarely do I just start off trying
to sell anyone anything guns blazing. There are some marketers and some markets where
you can get away with that. So I'm not saying to never do it. But for most of us, we have
to approach email with this same "be desire-less" attitude. Befriend them first. Talk about
an emotion, fear, desire, complaint, interest, etc they're thinking and talking about. Don't
start your email off saying, "hey you need to check out this new product... " or whatever.
Us some finesse.
Be a friend and confidant to them first (with no hint of sales man).
Then, after you've got their confidence you must ...
2. Be Excellent
Show them some skillz, Mr. Bad Boy.
-3-

-- 3 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
In the movie, the guy teaching these secrets would play the guitar or entertain kids
with women around so they could see his skillz. In his way of thinking, you have to show
some value, some reason why you're ... excellent. What makes you unique and interesting
and exciting?
Same with email.
I recommend doing this with teaching and entertainment.
(i.e. infotainment-- see the enclosed bonus training for more on this.)
Soft teaching, of course. And make them laugh or chuckle or at least smile. You can
also just inspire them, or get them thinking a bit differently about something. I do this as
much as possible in my emails and all you have to do is look at them to see how it's done.
But you definitely want to be excellent to your list. You need to show them why they
should not only read your email today, but why they should stick around and read it
tomorrow and, especially, why they should buy your product.
And how do you do that, you ask?
How do you get them to pull the trigger and buy?
Well, that brings us to the 3rd piece of the persuasion formula ...
3. Be Gone
The movie explains this with your typical "law of the jungle" analogy: "We pursue
that which retreats." In the game of meeting women, once you've gotten through their
"defenses" (by being desire-less) ... and then shown them why you're excellent and worthy
of their attention ... you now take it away!
Leave!
Make yourself inaccessible!
Create a situation where the woman is asking, ''Where's he at? Doesn't he find me
attractive and good enough???" instead of "here he is again .. .I wish this creep would
-4-

-- 4 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
leave me alone already. .. n In other words, don't make yourself such an easy "catchn --
after all, you're the stud with skillz and excellence. You're a rare commodity in a world of
chumps who are all desperate and needy. You're a fun guy, with something valuable to
bring to the table. Instead of you acting like all the other guys being needy and slobbering
over every woman in sight, you pull back, and make yourself the hunted, not the hunter.
Much easier to attract women that way.
Of course, this applies to selling in email, too.
It doesn't work exactly the same way, though.
But the idea is to make your product so good ... so appealing... so "must have' ...
people NEED to have it. And the way to "be gonen is to take it away. Don't let just
anyone buy. Don't let just anyone join. Put conditions on the purchase. Make it super
expensive (but still worth the value, of course). Slap a deadline on your offer (and make
sure they are painfully aware of it). Much of this is done on the landing page, not the
email. But you can also do a fair bit of this in the emails.
For example:
I LOVE telling people when products I'm selling are expensive.
That not just "anyone" can buy.
And that I will not even allow certain people to buy (and I really don't allow certain
people to buy my stu£1).
It's the email equivalent of being "gone." Of not making yourself so easy to buy
from, while everyone else is lobbing emails, tweets and face book messages at them
pressuring and sometimes even begging them to "click this link!" "buy this now!" "you
need this!"
Yada yada yada.
Doing emails in a way where you take it away not only can get you more sales
(assuming you MEAN it, and are not using it as a persuasion trick, which people can see
right through much of the time, as it feels manipulative when you don't have the right
-5-

-- 5 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
intention) but it can get you a higher quality of customer than you would normally get,
too.
So there you have it.
The Tao of email marketing.
Always remember... if you want to make more sales and close more deals, be desire-
less, be excellent, and then ... be gone. It's perhaps the simplest persuasion formula there
is. And it works like gangbusters.
''Grow Som.e Balls''
The above is written on a 3X5 card, taped right on the wall, staring at me every
single time I sit down at the computer. Why? And, more important, how does this put
more $$ in your hot little hand using email? I'm glad you asked, amigo. You see, a lot of
email marketing comes down to you sticking out in the inbox. You can do this any
number of ways. Like, for example, using the "Shock Effect'' subject line from time to
time. And also, writing about super controversial issues. Or by getting really personal in
an email where you let your entire list see a side of you or know something about you that
most people would not have the guts to do. These things may get you some flack or
whatever. But they also get you big attention. And, if you do it right, that attention will
translate into big sales.
But, let's face it:
Writing like this where you put yourself out on the chopping block ain't always easy.
It is easier for some than it is for others. And I often find myself second guessing an email
like this. Some recent examples would be when I talked about lessons from my divorce.
Or the email subject "50 shades of gay" or "How are you, you old bastard?" Those got
me a ton of feedback, and not all of it was good. And that's cool - I didn't care. What I
cared about was bonding with my list more and letting them know something about me
they didn't before, or shocking them out of their complacency with a little adventure
and/ or controversy to start their days with.
-6-

-- 6 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Then there's the shock effect subject lines. I'd cite one of mine, but honestly, the best
one I've seen in a long time is from "Email Players" subscriber Jon McCulloch who 'Wrote
an email with the subject line "The biggest cock I EVER saw".
That's gotta be one of my favorites.
And let's be honest here, for a lot of people, doing these sorts of things takes ballz to
send out. And thus, that's why I have that motto taped to my wall. Whenever I find myself
second guessing using a controversial subject line or writing about something
embarrassing or whatever (I've written about everything from having shy bladder to the
time I farted in study hall as a kid, it's all good) I look at that card and push that "send"
button.
So far I have only very rarely regretted it.
In fact, want to know something?
Over the past few years of writing Qiterally) thousands of cmails when you count my
own lists and lists of clients I've worked for, I've discovered it's actually pretty hard to push
too hard anymore. I don't know if society is just being desensitized to it, or the Internet
has kind of made these things less of a big deal (since everyone is out there swearing or
being crude on purpose these days just for effect). But more and more I agree with author
Peter Montoya when he said in an interview with Dan Kennedy many years ago:
"Chances are you aren't going far enough. But when you do, don't worry, your market
will let you know." (He may not have said those exact words, but that's the gist). And he's
right on. I sometimes push the envelope just for the fun of it and expect a pile of hate
mail, only to get just a few loose canons say some incoherent drivel about being offended
or whatever.
Anyway, so what is the point of all this?
Don't be afraid to write about things that are controversial.
That offend.
Or that aren't popular.
-7-

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
You might think you're pushing harcl. But chances are, you're not pushing hard
enough in your emails. So your assignment (if you choose to accept it!) for this month is to
write something SUPER controversial. Something you're afraid to write. Something that
makes you NERVOUS before pushing that "send" button.
I can't say for sure what will happen when you do.
And you should still use common sense and wisdom (so don't go nutzo here).
But my guess is, you'll make a few extra sales, and bond with your list more, while
getting far fewer complaints or unsubscribes than you expect.
You'll also show your list you're different from all the other emails they're getting.
And that you have (dare I say it?) a pair of ballz. (Figuratively, for ye ladies.)
All of which will make you more sales and position you stronger in your niche.
Speaking Of Ballz ...
While we're on the subject, I'm starting to get a little disappointed in some of the
emails I've seen from people who subscribe to this newsletter. Not in the content or
structure or subject lines... but in the sheepish and "soft" approach I'm seeing in a lot of
calls to action. It seems like many of y'all (not everyone) are still afraid to plug your links.
By that I mean, every. single. email. has a link. .. in the PS.
Almost like a whisper, instead of boldly telling them "hey, if you want to solve your
XYZ problem, you need to quit screwing around and click this link."
No good, boss.
No good at all.
You have to be more aggressive than the usual email wannabes who think the soft
sell is the best approach when it's not. And I know many people are scared of
unsubscribes (which is foolish, you need to get that out of your head if it's holding you

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
back) and looking like- GASP!- a "sales man." And that's fine. I don't necessarily want to
be seen as Mr. Sales Man, either. But, that doesn't mean I don't SELL, baby. And you
should, too. That is, if you want to start making more money with your emails.
But instead, what I keep seeing are these soft litde wimpy PS closes.
In other words ... they'll have done a great job in the email, but instead of closing the
email with a BANG, they close it with no close at all. Then, they pretty much whisper the
call to action (in a way totally disconnected and unrelated to the body of the email) in the
PS.
Now, I'm not saying to NEVER do that.
Sometimes you should do that, especially if you're in a hurry and are just getting
your email out and/ or can't think of a good way to tie the body copy into a strong close
naturally and organically. In those cases, I'll give you a pass. I do the same thing. But in
most cases you should begin with the end in mine. Start writing, and keep building
towards a strong close.
Look at 90% of my emails for examples.
They go for the close in the body copy (towards the end usually), not the PS.
But when I see these PS closes every time from people, I just want to reach in there
and crank that close up I 0 notches.
Don't be afraid!
If you did everything else right, your readers are LOOKING for a reason to buy.
So here's another assignment Qucky for you, when I give you assignments and
homework, they put money in your pocket!):
Your ne:xt 5 e~nails are NOT to have a PS close.
Instead, I want you to build towards a close before your sign off. Make it more of a
sales pitch. In fact, you know what a great formula is for emails? A formula that's decades

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
old and was created by (as far as I can tell) the greatjohn Caples? It's when you structure
your emails like this:
(I) Begin with content/ entertainment and no sales talk (be
desire-less!)
(2) Mix content/ entertainment with a little sales talk
(3) Segue into pure sales talk
"But Ben!" you may be asking. "What if I have the email written but just can't think
of some logical way to close the email? What then? Won't it look funny just putting a link
in there without it having any context?"
The answer:
Yes!
But don't worry about that. There have been times when I simply ended my email,
and inserted a link without ANY context. Were they my best performing emails? No. But
they beat the crap out of the ones where I hide them in a whispered PS. (Again, there
ARE times to do that, and I do them myself that way once in a while, but ideally you
want to plug it in the email, as people are becoming numb to PS's in many markets.) Hey,
when in doubt, just end your email with:
"To see how I solved my XYZ problem, go to: (LINK)"
Here's an example below. Notice how I don't build to a sale, just give soft
information away. But I gave my link context by using the above line:
Subject: Prostate myths that make you go blind
I 	love a good urban legend.
One of my favorites is masturbating makes you go blind or gives you
hairy palms 	(whenever telling someone that it's amusing how they'll
immediately look at their hands ••• )
Well, 	guess what?

And, of course, you can play with that last line (no pun) and say something like, "To

Jon McCulloch Rides Again
Remember last month when I said I'd start showing subscriber emails that kicked
gluteus assimus? Well, the first one up is from "Email Players" subscriber Joh McCulloch
(the guy I mentioned earlier who wrote that "cock" subject line). The following email
clocks in at over 900 words -- which is WAY longer than most emails are. And it goes to
show there are NO RULES. You do what you have to do to make the sale. In this email's
case, he sold at least 5 of his £697 product to a very small list. It was probably more sales
than that, because at least five people TOLD him this was "the one" (so I think it's safe to
say there were more who bought from this email and didn't say anything). Anyhoo, I
asked jon why he thought it pulled so well and he said:
It worked, I think, because:
1. It's an interesting, nay, fascinating, story.
2. It DEMONSTRATES what I'~n selling ("Hey... if this got ME sucked
in ... n1aybe if I write something like it, it'll suck MY readers in, too").
Take a look see -- try to dissect it and, most importantly, learn from it:
Subject: 	150ft up on a 6-inch sloping window ledge 	(true story)
Hi Jon,
Interesting email yesterday from someone who said, 	in effect, 	"I love
the way you write your emails but I 	could never do that because my
business is so boring and my readers aren't used to your kind of
approach".
Well ••• 	ummm ••• 	isn't this 	just another version of the old "but my
business is different" 	thing?

Let me share something with you, 	but bear with me first.
It's over 25 years ago now, 	but way back then I 	used to hang out with a
rum old crowd, 	and we spent most of our time smoking dope, 	fixing
motorcycles and listening to Motorhead.
Anyway, 	things change, 	as they do, 	and we all moved on a little and, 	to
be perfectly frank, 	some of us treated the drugs and stuff as the bit
of fun they were meant to be ••• 	but others got a bit deeper into it all
than they perhaps ought to have done.
As I 	recall, 	I was actually the only one holding down a steady job at
the time; 	not only that but I 	also won a kind of 	'scholarship' 	from my
employer to go to university, 	get my degree, 	and come back as a Bigwig.
My brain wasn't QUITE so addled I 	didn't realise that was a fine
opportunity, 	so I 	grabbed it with both hands.
The others were less fortunate. 	Some did get work and held down jobs,
but others ••• well, 	they kind of spiralled down into one big messy
shitpile.
So, 	at some point one of them, 	Tony, moved into a 15th floor flat on
one of the crappier estates in Leicester. And believe me, 	those were
then some seriously crap places to live. 	I 	don't now what they are like
now, 	but then they were so rough even the trees grew in twos.
I 	didn't go there too often myself, 	unless I was scoring some blow, 	but
one time I did drop into see him I 	vividly remember him showing me his
new trick (another time I went, 	one of his mates, 	Gus, was there drying
his urine-soaked jeans with an iron -- oh, 	the perils of drinking so
heavily you piss yourself while you're stupefied. The stench was
unbelievable) •
But that episode was not the worst of it, 	not by a long chalk (you
might want to sit down while you read this, 	because it could make you
feel slightly ill).
No, 	I was telling you about his new 	'trick', wasn't I?
See, 	he had this bloody great big window in his flat, 	and at each end
was a smaller one he could open. Gave a splendid view of the city from
its vantage point of 150 feet or so above the sprawling mess of
Highfields.

But Tony didn't use the window for looking out of; 	at least, 	not so far
as I 	know.
Because what he used to do 	(I swear I 	am not making this up) was •••
open the window on the left •••
climb out •••
walk along the narrow and cambered window cill •••
and climb back in the window on the right.
You've seen trailers for 	'Man on a Ledge"?
You've seen that old Twilight Zone feature?
Well, 	imagine that.
Only this is REAL.
And the guy is drunk, 	doped up to the eyeballs or on acid. 	He's
giggling away and having a right old time of it. And then his 	"friends"
occasionally would shut the windows and leave him out there for a
while.
Just for shits and grins, 	you know?
Did I 	tell you this was a 15th floor flat? 	I 	did, 	didn't I?
Jeeze. 	It sends me into a cold sweat just thinking about it. 	I'm not
particularly bothered by heights, 	but there is no way on this or any
other planet I'd shuffle along a 6in wide sloping ledge 150 feet in the
air for 	"fun".
Same guy around the same time was caught stealing a bottle of whiskey
from the Sainsbury's supermarket in the town centre.
The store detective chased him all the way through the city up to
Victoria Park, where he managed to lock himself in the public
toilets ••• and drink the entire bottle before Plod arrived.
So, what's the point I'm making here? What's the marketing message?
Well, 	there isn't one.

I
II
THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Nope. 	This email contain zero marketing content.
BUT it IS marketing in and of itself.
Let me explain •••
If you've read this far you were probably caught up in the 	'story'. 	You
wanted to hear what happened next.
You kept reading because whatever emotions you were feeling
curiosity, 	disgust, 	fear, 	excitement, 	incredulity, 	whatever
gripped to the point you didn't want to stop.
had you
And it had NOTHING to do with marketing or the services and products I
offer.
The underlying point is this: 	anyone could do this in any industry and
selling to any audience. 	Because it's storytelling, 	the finest kind of
communication between humans there is.
The idea your market or audience is 	"too sophisticated" is a 	load of
old tosh. 	No, 	not everyone will like it.
Some won't like the content; 	some won't like the delivery; 	some won't
like the "unprofessionalism"; 	and some will decide they don't like YOU,
personally.
But who cares?
Because by writing with your own personality
weaving through the tale like a golden thread, 	you will attract people
who DO like you -- and liking is a prerequisite to buying from you
(most of the time).
If you've read this far 	(and you must have done), 	then you have 	just
experienced first-hand the power of the kind of email marketing I'm
going to be sharing with you in Email supremacy:
http://www.jonmcculloch.com/email-supremacy
YOU, Jon, 	are exactly the kind of person I want joining me.
Warmly,
Jon

--- End Included Video ---
Good stuff, Mr. McCulloch! Study that bad-boy over and over, and let it influence
your emails. The storyI demonstration combo is powerful email writing. A lot of people
make this too complicated. But most emails that pull lots of loot in really are just simple
stories and interesting facts and demonstrations. (Just like a good sales pitch face-to-face
is). Anyway, jon's got lots of stuff you might find interesting going on. His website is at:
www.businesssupreJDacybulletin.coJD

Last month in part 1 of this series 	(taken from a long transcribed audio
training I 	did for the no-longer-for-sale $795.00 "Street Smart Email"
course) 	we talked about infotainment and how important 	(vital!) 	it is in your
marketing -- especially emails.
This time we're going to cover the first 7 	(of 21) 	ways to use infotainment.
(What I 	consider to be "the king ,of selling online".)
Enough of this clacking, 	let's hit it .••
[S'l'AR'l']
Infotainment Secret #1:
Inject your persona1ity into everything.
Inject your personality into all your sales and marketing pitches, 	even your
company literature, 	or just any contact you have. 	Inject your personality
into it. 	Again, 	this is one of those things that if you listen to the so-
called experts they'll tell you don't do that. 	You've got to be professional.
You've got to be a businessman or businesswoman and all that, 	and go out
the~e like some corporate robot and do it that way.
I 	just think that is absolute stupidity. 	The fact is, 	the world is starving
for entertainment. 	It's starving also for people to deal with. 	Nobody wants
to deal with a faceless bureaucratic machine or something.
Recently I 	had a company doing some transcribing for me, 	and they did a good
job, 	but my gosh, 	the person I was dealing with sounded like a robot. 	I mean
I 	could not relate to this person even through an email. 	Literally I 	felt
like I 	was talking to one of those automated voices on the telephone or
something. 	It was just really annoying and frustrating, 	so I dropped them.
I'm the same way. 	I 	can't stand dealing with these robotic-type organizations
and people, 	anything scripted. 	I don't want to deal with it. 	I want to deal
with a personality, 	a real person, 	someone who I 	can relate to, 	someone I can
sit down with and chat with, 	especially if you're trying to sell something to
them. 	It will just make you stick out from the crowd. 	People really are
seeking others with personality, 	whether they know it or not. 	If you give
someone a choice between dealing with a salesman or marketer who really
doesn't show their personality very much, 	and one who does and just wears it
on their sleeve, 	even if that person's a little obnoxious and a little
1
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 28 --

annoying, 	I 	guarantee you 9 out of ~0 times' they' 11 pick the personality over
the robot.
It's easy to do. 	Just be yourself! 	Use :words and slang- we're going to talk
about slang more and different words - 	but :Use yQur own figures of speech C!nd
your own way of talking. 	Be yourself! 	Don't worry~ people aren't going tro··· 	·
freak out on you because maybe you speak a little differently than them.
Maybe you're from the south and you' r.e dealing with. some Yankee up in the
north. 	It doesn't matter if they're used to it. Believe me, 	it will endear
people to you. 	Yes, 	there will be .a t:ew people wh.o have a problem with that,
but for the most part people appreciate :r~at cpeople.
I mean that when I 	say be yourself, 	warts and all. 	Don~t worry if you're not
perfect. 	You don't have to say things perfectly. 	If you were sitting down
talking to your own mom or writing an email to your own .mom or ~reating a
video or an audio podcast, 	or however you're selling, 	just pretend you're
dealing with someone you already know.
How would you talk to them? You'd loosen up like you're having a drink with
them. 	Don't act like you're drunk, 	but act like you're just having a laid
back conversation. 	People like that. 	One example - 	I've talked about this in
the Crackerjack Selling Secrets book - 	is Spiderman. 	I bring up comic books a
lot. 	It's a very easy way to kind of make a point. 	Here's a character who!s
been worth easily over $1 billion .to Marvel Comics in some shape or form.-
What makes it so there's five different titles of Spiderman that people buy
every month? It's not like there's just one comic. 	There's like five, 	and
then he guest stars in all these other ones, 	and·people feel compelled to
constantly buy Spiderman comics and watch the TV shows and the cartoons and
the movies.
I 	was once reading the biography of the guy who co-created Spiderman. 	The
writer who co-created him was Stan Lee. 	In his day he created a lot of comic
characters. 	He was saying that when he was writing those stories, 	his biggest
challenge writing Spiderman had nothing to do with what v'illain do I. use and
what's the gimmick I'm going to use, 	how am I going to put his life in
danger, 	and all that. 	It's none of that stuff you would think.
His biggest problem was finding more personal problems for Peter Parker to
deal with, 	which is Spiderman's alter ego. 	He wanted to make him like a kid
that anybody could relate to. 	He gave him a real personality with real
problems. 	At that age it seems like every problem is like the end of the
world, 	and he did a great job of it. 	People will tell you, 	I 	guarantee, 	if
you ever have a chance to talk with or if you read interviews of anyone who
creates comics, 	the artists and the writers who get paid the most money in
that industry, 	they will tell you, 	~Yeah, 	I 	used to collect Spiderman, 	but
really I wanted to see what was going on with Peter Parker's love life,
what's going on with his new girlfriend and that sort of thing."
This is even a reflection of the second Spiderman movie, 	which grossed an
awful lot of money and was considered the best of the three, 	not just by the
box office receipts, 	but by the fans and everything, 	because it was actually
more about Peter Parker than it was about Spiderman. 	It was about a
personality. 	People like that.
Another example would be talk radio. We talked about Rush Limbaugh earlier,
but think of all the talk radio. 	All the big talk radio shows, 	almost every

single one of them, 	is more or less entertainment. 	I 	don't care if you're

Money is being generated because of an entertaining/infotaining personality,

Infotainment Secret 12:
Tell Stories
This might seem obvious, 	but you'd be amazed how few people use stories. 	It's
a great way to sell anything. 	I 	don't care what the medium is, 	it doesn't
matter, 	if you can work a story in in some way, 	shape, 	or form, 	and you
should any time you can.
What makes stories so powerful? First of all, 	they're naturally entertaining.
Think back thousands of years ago. 	Kings and queens and that sort of thing,
this was their entertainment, 	some guy coming in and telling stories, 	or the
guy at the local tavern. 	Before they had TV, 	just some guy would tell
stories. 	It's just a natural fohn of entertainment. 	It's very easy to process
information from stories too. 	We're almost hard wired for it.
In a lot of ways, 	we're hard wired for entertainment because we're hard wired
to understand and tell stories, 	all of us, 	whether we know it or not. 	We all
have the ability to tell stories, 	and you just have to practice it.
Again, 	it's very easy for people to process stories. 	It's very easy for
people to remember stories. 	In fact, 	I 	once took a memory training class back
when I 	was in college because I 	just didn't want to do a lot of hard
studying, 	which is even true today. A lot of people don't like studying a lot
of stuff, 	which is why just plain flat-out hard content usually turns people
off in a lot of cases.
This memory training thing, 	one of the things they told us is that you can
remember things, 	events, 	or facts into a story form, 	which is very
interesting. 	He went through the Bill of Rights and he just put it into a
story form, 	and it was very, 	very powerful. 	It just shows you how our brains
work.
Stories also have a viral effect that people just love spreading good
stories. 	If you look at social media, 	for example - 	Twitter, 	Facebook, 	blogs,
really anywhere, 	even in the news- a good story spreads. 	There's different
ways of telling stories and all that sort of thing, 	but basically you want to
tell stories that are entertaining, 	they spread, 	people like hearing them,
and they can deliver your sales information.
They also have a lot of lasting power. A good story will be around for a very
long time. 	Think of the Christmas story, 	for example, 	or the story of the
3
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 28 --

independence of the United Stijltes. Most,people have been told these stories
and they remember them and they don't really forget them. 	They could probably
tell them back to people in their sleep.
Even if they don't celebrate Christmast a lot of people know the Christmas
story because they heard it and it was ;n· a story form. 	It has lastingpower,.
and can last thousands of years in some. eases.
Here's something very interesting. A lot of times sales are made just solely
on a story. 	I know that there are cert.ain products that I've bought, 	whether
it's through an ad or other mechanisms, 	whera I was just sold by hearing the
story of how the product came about, 	or the story of someone who used the
product. 	I'm sold before I even get to. the benefits. 	I was entertained by the
story that delivered information, 	which was infotainment, 	and it got me.
I 	can tell you this as someone who writes sales letters, 	I 	always work a
story into everything because of that. 	I 	just know, 	based on tests I've seen
and that sort of thing, 	that you take that story out and sales will plummet,
because it's really doing a lot of the work for you.
There's some great examples of storytellers. 	Some of these people you may not
want to hear, 	but 	just trust me, 	they're very good storytellers. One would be
talk show radio host, 	Michael Savage. 	Now I 	personally don't listen to his
show a lot, 	because I 	just don't have time for ·it and sometimes it gets a
little depressing, 	but at the same time it's extremely entertaining. 	He's in
the top 10 most listened to radio shows out there. 	He just knows how to tell
a story.
If you did nothing but just take his stories and just kind of read them or
listen to them, 	you will become really good at this. 	I've gotten inspired
many times by just listening to him.
Another good storyteller would be Robert Kiyosaki. 	He's the author of the
Rich Dad, 	Poor Dad books. 	You may have read it, 	or may not have, 	but I'm
pretty sure most people have heard of Robert Kiyosaki. 	He also has a column
on Yahoo, 	if you don't .want to buy any of his books. 	He has a column on
Yahoo.com and you'll have to look for it on there.
He always illustrates his points with a good story. 	You're not getting some
bland, 	boring, 	cold, 	dry, 	stilted economic analysis or anything like that,
because that's his thing- finance and economics. 	You're getting a good story
and it just entertains you in a great way.
Jesus Christ was a storyteller. 	He told a lot of His teachings, 	if not all of
them, 	in a parable format. 	In fact, 	I believe it even says that in the Bible,
that everything He taught was in a parable. Why would he do that? There's a
book called The 7 Lost Secrets of Success by Joe Vitale, 	which is about a guy
named Bruce Barton, 	who was another good storyteller by the way, 	an old
advertising guy. 	He once said it would have been easy, 	for example, 	for Jesus
just to say, 	"Be a good neighbor," when he was telling a story about being a
good neighbor. 	Instead He started the story with, 	"Some guys were walking
down this road and they got assaulted by thieves." He was telling a story
first, 	an entertaining story at that, 	especially one that people of that time
could actually relate to and they could kind of step into that story.
Something you want to do with your stories, 	by the way, 	is you want to make
it so people feel 	like they're a part of it, 	and Jesus did that. 	He was a
4
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

master at it. 	It doesn't matter if you believe in Jesus or not, 	either. 	Just
read His stories for no other reason than to learn storytelling. 	It's a great
way of doing it.
Another one is Gary Halbert. 	He's now deceased as of a few years ago, 	but he
was just a master at telling stories in his ads. 	Even when he was doing
podcasts and teaching, 	he was so good at infotainment. 	Again, 	I 	don't know if
he did it on purpose and consciously, 	or if he just being himself with his
own personality, 	which is infotainment as we've seen, 	but he was great at
this.
He would tell stories in ads where you were, 	like I 	said before, 	sold on the
product before you even knew what the product was in some cases. 	That's how
powerful a story is. 	If you can ever get your hands on some Gary Halbert ads
-his sons have a website up for him at www.TheGaryHalbertLetter.com- you'll
see this. 	He was just a master at it.
Again, 	we talked about Bruce Barton before. 	He was another one. 	Anything you

So that's infotainment secret t2 - 	stories.
Infotainment Secret #3:
Unusual language
This is another thing I 	learned from observing Matt Furey, 	who I 	told you
about earlier. 	There's something about unusual language. 	I'm not talking
about swearing or anything like that. 	I'm saying unusual language that you
wouldn't normally expect to hear. 	It just sticks in the mind, 	almost like
spackle. 	It's just like you can't get it out of your head.
In fact, 	one of the people I've interviewed is a guy named Jim Yaghi, 	who's a
pay per click guy, 	a computer scientist and just a genius of AdWords. 	He was
telling me, 	and this is in that interview, 	that a lot of times he'll write
headlines that are just kind of wacky, 	kind of unusual, 	something you
wouldn't expect to see in a Google Adwords. 	He would just put that in the
headline and he'd get a lot higher responses.
It's something to test, 	obviously, 	but it does prove the point. 	All these
infotainment things can be used even for something like pay per click. 	It
just makes you naturally entertaining.
I'll give you some examples. 	A friend of mine named Doberman Dan Gallapoo, 	I
was talking to him once and out of the whole conversation, 	which was like two
years ago, 	the only thing I 	remember for sure was one thing he said. 	We were
talking about copywriting and just how important is copywriting, 	because
remember, 	it's not king, 	no matter what the chest-beating copywriters might
say.
He said this and I 	never forgot it. 	~Ben, brilliant copy will not sell a
turd." That's ·kind of a very strange way of saying it, 	but it got the point
across and I've never forgotten it in two years. 	That's how powerful it is
just saying something in an unusual way, 	and it's kind of entertaining.
Recently I 	wrote a blog post with one way of saying, 	~Be careful, 	watch your
butt," or ~watch your ass," some people say. 	I 	found out another way of
saying those words, 	"gluteus maximus." I 	said, 	"Watch your gluteus assimus."
5
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 28 --

It's just unusual, 	it's weird, 	and someone may not even know what that means,
but it just sticks in your head.
Another example, 	and I believe this might have been in the same blQg post,
I'm just giving you a recent example, 	I said something about, 	~Look, here's
how dumb this business was. 	If you looked up the definition of 	'big dumb
stupid business mistake,' 	you'd probably see a picture of the place that I
saw this blunder take place."
I was talking about a coffee shop that basically ~c;t.S sending their
competition customers because they kept S(i!ndin9 them to an ATM machine that
was closer to another coffee shop because they didn't take cash. 	I 	said, 	~If
you looked up the definition of an idiot business, 	a picture of this place
would be right next to it." It's just weird, 	it's unusual, 	but it just sticks
in the head.
Another way to use unusual language for infotainment would be intentional
misspellings. 	I mentioned Matt Furey earlier. 	He's a pro at this. 	He's great
at this. 	With email it's very important because yo~ want to get past spam
filters, 	and some words like 	'money' 	and stuff you do want to misspell
anyway, 	but he quoted a writer named Mark Twain, 	which I'm pretty sure
everyone listening to this has heard of or could easily get access to who he
was. 	He was a great novelist.
Mark Twain had this quote. 	He basically said, 	~I don't give a damn about any
man who only knows how to spell a word one way." We hear all these grammar
rules and everything, 	and that's why when you do something that breaks a
rule, 	it naturally gets attention. 	People are naturally interested in it.
It's entertaining in a way. 	·
I've done this myself. 	I've misspelled words like 	'expert.' 	I might say "ex-
spurt' 	just because it's weird, 	it just stands out. 	A lot of people like to
use the word 	'guru' 	in direct marketing. Anyone who's listening to this who's
in direct marketing hears, 	~Here's what the gurus are doing." That means
basically the guys who are sort of at the top of the food chain in marketing.
I'll kind of make fun of that word. 	Instead of saying 	'guru' 	I'll say 	'goo-
roo.' 	Why? 	It kind of sticks out. 	It also kind of positions me as someone
who's an anti-guru in some ways. 	It's almost like mocking the word. 	I'm not
necessarily mocking gurus. 	I'm just saying that it stands out. 	In fact, 	I've
seen other people on my list using that word now in their emails and when
they're talking on forums and stuff, 	and that's fine. 	It doesn't matter to
me.
Another word that I've used to misspell would be 	'skillz,' 	because it's got
that z sound to it. Again it just gets attention. 	It stands out and it's kind
of entertaining to see a word misspelled on purpose.
'Stupid' 	would be another one. 	I 	do this all the time, 	'stoopid.' 	Or the word
'dog.' 	A lot of times I'll spell it 	'dawg.' 	If I want to say, 	~He kicked
someone's butt," which is a common phrase here in American slang, 	I might say
'boo-tay.' 	Again at first glance on its own nobody would know what it means,
but in the context of the sentence that I use these words in, 	it's fine, 	it's
clear, 	and it gets attention.
I've seen Matt Furey use words like instead of spelling money like m-o-n-e-y
he might spell it m-u-n-e-y. 	Instead of TV he'll actually spell out t-e-e-v-
6
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 28 --

•
e-e. 	Instead of using the word 	'million,' 	he'll say m-i-1-1-y-u-n. Again,
that's a good tactic to get around spam filters that are sometimes screening
out words like million.
I'm not saying to take these exact words, 	and I wouldn't recommend taking
these exact words and going out and using them and everything. 	That's not the
point. 	You want to find your own peculiar way of saying things. 	Find your own
style out there. 	Don't copy me. 	You'll ruin it for both of us, 	because people
will look at you as a wanna-be, 	and the stuff I do will hav~'less impact, 	so
just find your own way of doing it. 	It's not hard.
Another way of using unusual language for infotainment is we talked about
slang. 	People love listening to slang. 	In fact, 	some people get kind of
worried - 	~People in other countries won't understand my slang." You would be
surprised. 	People in other countries like the slang from other countries. 	I
love listening to slang from like England or Australia. 	I 	just love it. 	I
can't get enough of it! 	It's so different and unusu~l and interesting, 	and
yes, 	entertaining to hear. 	So use slang. 	Use your own slang. 	Don't worry
about people getting mad or anything like that. Anyone who does probably
isn't a good prospect anyway, 	so use slang.
Finally, 	sound bites. You see politicians using sound bites all the time, 	and
there's a chapter in Crackerjack Selling Secrets about this if you want some
examples. 	Definitely use sound bites when appropriate. 	This is how a lot of
politicians will actually get big bills passed or win the votes of people who
actually think they're lying scammers, 	just because of a good sound bite.
They're very powerful.
Infotainment Secret #4:
Paraphrasing Popular Quotes
There's a lot of quotes floating around. 	There's quotes that you've heard
probably over and over and over. 	There's quotes that I've definitely heard
over and over and over. 	They're just kind of ingrained in our minds and our
psyches, 	when we hear certain quotes.
Let's face it, 	most quotes are kind of dry, 	especially when they're older
quotes that maybe an ex-President said or something. 	They might be kind of
dry, 	told in a dry way, 	but there's a way you can kind of use them to make
them entertaining. 	You can take other people's quotes and kind of play around
with the words to say something completely different, 	to kind of give them a
twist. 	By giving them this twist and putting in your own thought into their
quote, 	you're not really stealing their quote, 	you're just kind of playing
off it and paraphrasing it.
Just for some examples, 	there's this video from Earl Nightingale called The
Strangest Secret. 	Actually this was from the audio version. 	It was an album
recorded I 	think in the late 50's. 	He said something about, 	"If a man thinks
about nothing, 	he becomes nothing." 	It's an Earl Nightingale saying.
One time I 	was writing about swipe files in my daily email, 	and I 	said, 	"If
we read about crap, 	we write about crap," which is basically saying if you
read bad ads, 	you're going to write bad ads.
Another example would be Zig Ziglar, 	the famous sales trainer, 	a very good
author and lecturer and just really good at selling. 	He once had a quote that
a lot of people have heard and requited, 	saying, 	~Timid salesmen have skinny
7
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 28 --

kids," which by itself is a pretty 99Qd. entertaining quote. 	One time I
applied that to copywriting. 	I 	said, 	"Lazy copywriters have skinny kids" -
lazy copywriters who don't do the work and do the necessary research and that
sort of thing.
There was a popular quote that one of fq~elr President Clinton's advisors
said. 	"It's the economy, 	stupid." The qtiOtE;! ,basically was saying that's all
people cared about is the economy, 	and you've got to.focus on that. 	"It's the
economy, 	stupid." I put that into marketing terms once in one of my emails,
and I 	said, 	"It's the market, 	stupid." I 	ju.!3t. ~ind of paraphrased what
someone else said in my own words.
Then finally, 	this is kind of a recent example, 	a 	lot of people whether
they've read the Bible or not have probably heard the phrase, 	"A wolf in
sheep's clothing" -basically a wolf pretending to be a sheep and it's very
dangerous. 	I 	kind of played around with the words and called certain people,
"Newbies in guru clothing" - 	the idea being that people who are kind of just
brand new to marketing or something. 	They're really brand new. Maybe they've
read a book or two and they're out there acting like they're top experts and
that sort of thing, 	giving often bad advice.
You can play around with any kind of quote like that. 	Just paraphrase part of
the quote. 	People find it very fun to read. 	It's entertaining, 	it's thought
provoking, 	and it makes something that may have been otherwise a dry subject
more interesting.
Before we move on to the next thing, 	I do want to give you a warning. 	I 	do
not think you should be copying things verbatim all the time and acting like
you said it, 	especially from people who are still alive and that sort of
thing. 	Just play on the words. 	You don't ever have to copy. 	Just play on the
words and you'll be far more entertaining and interesting anyway.
Infotainment Secret #5:
Be funny
Now here's something that I 	guarantee you you're not going to hear a 	lot from
the so-called marketing establishment. A lot of people will tell you to avoid
doing this. Again, 	I 	keep going back to Matt Furey. 	I 	kind of feel I 	was
given permission to do this after I 	studied him.
Be funny! 	I'm not saying to do it all the time, 	and there are places where
you shouldn't do it. 	Like I would not recommend just telling outright jokes
in a direct mail sales letter or anything like that, 	or to a cold market
who's never heard of you and doesn't know you. 	For the most part I 	just
wouldn't do that.
But in most cases where you're selling- I'm talking about in articles or
emails - 	when you're talking to your people, 	people who are kind of like your
fans, 	it's okay to crack a joke now and then, 	especially if it's funny.
Laughter really does kind of open people's minds. 	It kind of makes them ~pen
and receptive to whatever else you have to say.
In fact, 	from what I 	understand, 	a lot of times people at seminars or public
speakers will open with a 	joke just because of that. 	It kind of gets people
primed and loosened up and they're more receptive. 	They're not really on
guard. 	It's a great way to do it. 	You don't even have to make them laugh.
Just make people smile or chuckle, 	and there's a lot of ways. Again, 	if

you're just using your own personality and your own slang and your own words,
misspelling words every now and then on purpose to make a point, 	trust me,
you're going to make people smile, 	if nothing else, 	every now and then. 	And
that's what you want to do.
People are looking for entertainment, 	like we were saying, 	but they're also
looking for someone to make them laugh, 	which is entertainment in a form. 	It
helps people bond with you better. 	I mean let's face 	it, 	any women listening
to this could probably tell you better than I 	could, 	but a 	lot of women like
guys who just make them laugh. 	They could be maybe not the most attractive
people' to that particular woman or whatever, 	but they like people who laugh.
We're the same way as guys. 	We're always looking for things that make us
laugh. 	We just get entertained by different things usually than women do, 	but
the point is if you can make someone laugh and smile, 	they'll bond with you.
It'll attract people to you. 	People want to be around the life of the party,
the person who's having a good time and is funny.
Again, 	people are seeking this out. 	They may not know they're seeking it out,
they may not tell you they're seeking it out, 	but believe me, 	they're s~eking
it out. 	You and I 	are both seeking it out. We love good funny things. 	Unless
somebody's got something against that- and those people are few and far
between- it's okay. 	Don't worry about it. 	It's fine.
There are lots of ways to be funny. 	Again, 	this isn't going to apply to
everything, 	so I do want to warn you about that. 	One way to do it would be
again to tell a 	joke. 	It could be your joke or someone else's joke, 	it
doesn't really matter. 	The point is, 	people like to tell a good joke. 	They
like to pass a good joke on. 	If you're the one telling it, 	that's good. 	It's
entertaining.
Use funny words. We talked about misspelling words already, 	which can be kind
of funny and entertaining. 	Let me think of a good example. 	I've mentioned
Matt Furey just because he's so good at this. 	I guess him or his brother, 	one
of the two, 	came up with a name for people who don't exercise, 	because he
sells to people into fitness and exercise. 	He came up with a word
'lardassian,' 	which is like the word 	'lardass' 	kind of mixed with this race
from the Star Trek movies called the Cardassian, 	and he kind of merged it.
It's kind of a funny word. 	Even if you don't know what it means, 	it's kind of
funny.
Make fun of a common enemy. 	If you've established some kind of common enemy
with your market, 	the people you're selling to, 	if you both dislike something
- 	like for example, 	I'm pretty much against most news. 	I 	think most news is
negative. 	I 	can't stand listening to it. 	I 	think it does more harm than good.
I 	think it hurts people psychologically and emotionally and all kinds of
ways. 	It keeps people scared. 	I 	don't like it.
So one time I 	created this video just to kind of keep people on their toes. 	I
just I 	kind of just said it. 	Again, 	I've been practicing this infotainment
for a long time now. 	Instead of listening to these newscasters, 	I 	said,
"Instead of listening to these nightly nitwits on the news_" - 	I 	just kind of
came up with my own name of doing it. 	I 	remember a couple people even wrote
to tell me they laughed out loud when they read it, 	so it's kind of funny.
Make fun of yourself. 	Again, 	you're a normal person, 	I'm a normal person,
we're not perfect. 	If you've done something or you made a big mistake that
you can kind of use to illustrate a point or teach someone how to sell

something with, 	use it! 	It's okay to make fun of yourself. 	Don't beat
yourself up or anything, 	don't dwell oh it, 	but people like that.
Another thing is just tell them using stories. 	This is probably the best way
of doing it. Oftentimes a good amusing story will get people laughing more
than a joke or anything else. 	I'll give you an example. 	Several months ago I
wrote this email, 	which I 	titled "Liars are buyers." Basically I 	told the
story of how this guy I 	used to work with many years ago - 	this was right
after the second George Bush election - 	he basically was on this cruise ship
with his wife. 	He'd won some cruise and they were·standing next to these two
guys who were laying out in the sun with sunglasses on, 	just enjoying it and
relaxing, 	like people do on cruises.
The one guy who was laying there, 	without even changing his expression or
anything, 	he was like, 	"I don't know what we're going to do now that Bush got
back in there. 	I 	don't know what we're going to do." The guy was just kind of
laying there all relaxed and didn't even like change his facial expression or
anything. 	Apparently that night my friend saw him kind of out partying and
stuff, 	having a good time, 	and how distressed was he? It was 	just kind of
funny, 	the image he gave of that - 	these two guys just kind of sitting there
all calm and cool and collected- "I don't know what we're going to do," like
their whole life is about to fall apart or something, 	yet they're sitting on
a cruise ship enjoying it.
Anyway, 	that was kind of an amusing story and I got a lot of feedback on it:
A lot of people found that very funny. 	In fact, 	I was talking to a guy who
produces radio shows and stuff, 	and he'd read it and got a big kick out of it
and that sort of thing.
So be funny. 	Again, 	before we move on to the next thing, 	just one word of

common sense and always put your market first. 	Know what they're feeling,
what they're thinking. 	There's sometimes you don't necessarily want to do
this, 	so I'd say use common sense and you'll be fine.
Infotainment Secret #6:
Be witty.
Again, 	people like fun people. 	They also like people who are witty, 	people
who can go with the flow. 	You see this sometimes in old presidential debates.
I was a kid at the time, 	but I 	still remember when Ronald Reagan was debating
Jimmy Carter. 	He'd go, 	"There you go again." It was 	just kind of like this
witty comeback, 	probably to a good question. 	I'm not judging either
candidate, 	but it was pretty funny, 	even as a kid. 	People like that, 	they
like wit. 	It makes people smile. 	Again, 	if you can just get people cracking a
smile, 	they'll be more bonded to you. 	It endears people to you. 	They like
that. 	It almost makes them feel safe to be around you, 	like they just feel
good being around you or hearing from you.
This is one of those things where I 	say not everything is going to work for
everybody. 	Some people are just not naturally witty people. 	Some are very
good at this and some people just have a hard time with it, 	but I would say
this. 	If you want to learn how to do it and kind of observe people who are
witty and who don't even realize in some cases that they're being witty,
there's several things to do.

There's politically charged blogs. 	I 	don't care if it's right wing or left
wing. 	Find these politically charged wings. 	One that I 	like to go to is
www.voxday.blogspot.com. 	That's a good one. 	Huntington Post has a lot of
charged people on there. 	Look at the blog posts - 	not the writer's stuff
necessarily, 	although you can if they're good witty people -but look at the
people who respond to these blogs. 	They're very, 	very good at wording things
and having snappy comebacks. 	They're just witty. 	I'm not saying you want to
learn to put people down or anything, 	but there's a wit. 	It's a sharp wit,
like you have just the right thing to say to kind of make a point in a quick
fashion, 	and people just love it. 	They admire it.
Another good example of this would be on Twitter. 	There's somebody that I
recommend everybody follow if you're on Twitter, 	and it's a guy named Blair
Warren. 	You can find him on www.Twitter.com/blairwarren. 	Everything he writes

whatever you're selling. 	If you're doing some kind of written sales where
you're not on the spot, 	this is great. 	You can do this all day long.
Another writer who's pretty good at wit is Ann Coulter. 	Again, 	I'm not
necessarily her biggest fan, 	but no matter what you think about her, 	the
woman's witty. 	All her writings are just witty. 	You can read her columns
right on the internet for 	free and it's a good example.
Finally, 	old comedies, 	especially Bob Hope comedies I've found. 	His
characters all had a certain wit about them. 	Again, 	I'm not saying you've
always got to do it and take it to the degree they took it, 	but people like
to be around witty people. 	They like to hear from them, 	they like to
communicate with them, 	so I 	suggest if you want to use this in your
infotainment quiver, 	so to speak, 	it's a great arrow to have.
Infotainment Secret #7:
Unpredictability
This is huge, 	and anybody can do this. 	Just realize this - 	predictability is
boring. 	It always has been, 	it always will be. 	Nobody likes the predictable.
Even people who think they like things to be predictable are bored to death
by predictability. No matter how you're selling, 	it really doesn't matter.
Don't be so predictable. 	·
Again, 	we were talking about using unusual words and that sort of thing and
using your personality. 	All these things are kind of unpredictable. 	Nobody's
expecting you to do it, 	but predictability in itself is very boring.
Being a guy, 	I 	can only tell this from a guy's point of view, 	but it's kind
of like romancing a woman. 	Women love it when you're kind of unpredictable.
They like being sent flowers when they're not expecting it, 	or being taken to
interesting places when they're not expecting it. 	·
This applies no matter who you're selling to. 	They like to kind of have
unpredictable things go on, 	fun unpredictable things - 	obviously not
something that's going to make their life harder, 	but interesting and
pleasantly unpredictable thi~gs. You always want to do that.
You always want to be changing things up constantly, 	especially if you're
connecting with your market and your customers regularly like I do. 	I email

-- 27 of 28 --

five days a week usually. 	You don't want to say the same thing over and over
and over, 	or talk about the same thing pver and over and over.
This is something even the great copywriter, 	Eugene Schwartz, 	discussed. Most
people in copywriting would know who he is. He was a very popular copywriter
back in his day, 	and he's no longer with ~s, but in his book, 	Breakthrough
Advertisin,g he talked about keeping things unpredictable. 	You don't want to
bore anybody.
Boredom is like the kiss of.death in sales, 	which is one of the reasons
infotainment and entertainment is so great. 	If you're unpredictable in your
words and ideas and the way you say things and everything, 	it's just fun to
read. 	It's entertaining.
I 	guess one way to illustrate this would be like cereal boxes. 	You can have
the same bowl of cereal every day or you can get one of those multi-pack
cereal boxes where you 	might have Corn Pops one day or Frosted Flakes the
next day or Cocoa Puffs the next day. 	It's just different. 	People like that.
They like that unpredictability.
One good example of this would be TV. One TV show I 	like to watch is called
Smallville, 	which is about Superman, 	ie Clark Kent, 	as a teenager before he
became Superman. 	Some of the popular episodes were about red kryptonite,
which in the show kind of made Clark a sociopath. 	If you know the pop culture
story of Superman, 	you know that green kryptonite hurts him and weakens him
and kills him, 	but this red kryptonite, 	at least in the show - 	for all you
comic book fans out there, 	I 	know this isn't the way it is in the comics -
but in the show the red kryptonite basically turns Clark into like a
sociopath, 	almost like a kid on drugs. 	He doesn't care about what he does.
He'll share his secret with anyone. 	He works for the mob and goes out and
marries some girl in Vegas and just does weird stuff, 	unpredictable things .•
These shows are extremely popular. 	They had really good ratings. 	They haven't
done one in the last couple years for some reason, 	but I do know at the time
the ratings always go really high when they know the red kryptonite episode
is going to be on.
But had they done red kryptonite episodes every week, 	it would have got
boring really., 	really fast. 	There would have been a backlash. 	People would
get bored and go somewhere else, 	and who could blame them? They've been too
predictable. But keeping things unpredictable is always interesting.
Other good TV shows do the same thing. 	They don't have the same plot every
week. 	They might have the same plot formula, 	but they make it different and
interesting and change it up. 	So unpredictability is 17.
